lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — MRSA: methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
Staphylococcus aureus is one of the most important bacterial pathogens of humans, full stop.
It causes skin and soft tissue infections, bloodstream infections, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia, and a frankly absurd number of post-surgical infections.
It colonizes the nostrils and skin of around 30% of healthy adults asymptomatically, ready to cause trouble if it gets into a break in the barrier.
It is, in normal life, kept in check by penicillin-family drugs — when those work.
Half of this lecture is about what happens when they don't.
MRSA is Staph aureus that carries the mecA gene, encoding PBP2a, the alternative penicillin-binding protein with low affinity for essentially all clinically available beta-lactams.
The defining mechanism is target modification — the cell wall is being built by a transpeptidase the drug cannot bind.
We covered the mechanism last segment; here I want to give you the epidemiology and history.
The mecA gene is carried on a mobile genetic element called SCCmec — staphylococcal cassette chromosome mec.

SCCmec is a piece of DNA that inserts into a specific site in the Staph chromosome and carries mecA along with various accessory genes, including the cassette chromosome recombinase genes that excise and insert it.
There are multiple SCCmec types — I, II, III, IV, V, and so on — that differ in size and gene content.
Types I, II, and III are the larger, more complex cassettes, historically associated with hospital-acquired MRSA.
Types IV and V are smaller, more mobile, and associated with the community-acquired MRSA strains that have emerged since the late 1990s.
The MRSA timeline.
1959: methicillin introduced clinically.
1960: first methicillin-resistant Staph aureus isolate reported by Patricia Jevons from a UK hospital.
1960s-1970s: MRSA remains uncommon, mostly a sporadic hospital problem.
1980s: MRSA epidemics in hospitals begin in earnest, particularly in the US and UK.
By the early 1990s, in some US hospitals, more than half of all Staph aureus isolates from intensive care units are MRSA.

Late 1990s-2000s: emergence of community-acquired MRSA — CA-MRSA — strains that cause infections in otherwise healthy people in the community, often presenting as severe skin and soft-tissue infections.
The dominant US CA-MRSA strain is USA300, which carries SCCmec type IV and also a toxin called Panton-Valentine leukocidin that may contribute to its virulence.
USA300 spread across the US in the 2000s with extraordinary speed.
In the US today, roughly 120,000 invasive MRSA infections per year, with on the order of 10,000-20,000 attributable deaths.
Globally, MRSA accounts for one of the largest individual contributions to AMR mortality.
In some Asian and Latin American hospitals, MRSA proportions among Staph aureus isolates run 70% or higher.
In Northern Europe — Netherlands, Scandinavia — aggressive screening and decolonization programs have kept hospital MRSA proportions below 5%.
So the variation between countries is enormous, and it tracks not the bacterial biology but the public-health and infection-control investment.
The treatment of MRSA, when it gets into the bloodstream or causes serious invasive disease, defaults to vancomycin.
Vancomycin is a large glycopeptide that targets the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, blocking transpeptidation at a completely different point from the beta-lactams.
Vancomycin doesn't care whether you have PBP2a — it's binding the substrate, not the enzyme.
So vancomycin works against MRSA.
For now.

Other options include daptomycin (a lipopeptide that disrupts the cell membrane), linezolid (an oxazolidinone that binds the bacterial ribosome at a unique site), ceftaroline (a fifth-generation cephalosporin specifically designed to bind PBP2a — yes, finally), and a small number of others.
I want to draw your attention to one frightening twist before we move on.
Vancomycin-intermediate Staph aureus — VISA — appeared in 1996.
These are strains with thickened cell walls that essentially absorb vancomycin in the outer layers before it can reach its target.
And then, in 2002, the first vancomycin-resistant Staph aureus** — VRSA — was reported in Michigan.
The mechanism in VRSA is acquisition of the vanA operon — the same operon that confers vancomycin resistance in enterococci, which we'll cover in the very next segment.
The gene appears to have jumped from a co-infecting vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus to the Staph inside the same patient.
VRSA is, mercifully, still rare — only a few dozen cases reported globally as of the 2020s.

But it is the worst-case scenario realized at the molecular level: the last-resort drug for the most important resistant gram-positive pathogen has been defeated, by a gene borrowed from a different genus, in a single human patient's body.
Hold onto that "borrowed from a different genus" detail, because it is the perfect setup for our next segment.
